name: env-prefix-mutator-deny
doc: Closes the env-prefix / transparent-wrapper bypass on code mutators (0.1.60). Before the fix, bash-write.ts short-circuited on any SAFE_PREFIXES first token (`env `, `cp `, `timeout `-adjacent) BEFORE testing CODE_MUTATORS, so `env sed -i src/x.ts` was silently ALLOWED (bash-write-patterns.ts documented it as a 'known, accepted gap'). Now CODE_MUTATORS + CODE_COMMAND_WRITE are CMD-anchored (bash-command-anchor.ts) and run BEFORE the short-circuit, while quoted/argument mentions still fall through. Step 1: `env sed -i` on a code file → deny (mutator behind a transparent wrapper). Step 2: `cp a b; tee <code>.ts` (tee to a code file behind a safe `cp ` prefix, past no decoy) → deny. Step 3: a quoted mutator mention inside an `npm run` arg → allow (empty): not a command position. Step 4: `echo 'patch file'` (patch quoted, not a command token) → allow (empty). Unit-covered by test/bash-write.test.ts.

steps.doc: `env sed -i` on a code file: the `env ` first token is a SAFE_PREFIX, but CODE_MUTATORS now runs FIRST — CMD folds the `env` wrapper into the anchor, `sed\b[^|]*\s-i` matches → blockCodeWrite "sed in-place edit". Assert the deny envelope.

`cp a b; tee <code>.ts`: `cp ` is a SAFE_PREFIX so the whole line used to short-circuit to allow. CODE_COMMAND_WRITE now matches the `tee` targeting a `.ts` after the `;` separator → blockCodeWrite "tee/dd into a code file". Assert the [BLOCKED] tag survives.

A quoted mutator MENTION in an npm arg: `sed -i` sits inside a double-quoted `--grep` value, never at a command position, so CMD anchoring does not match; `npm run` is a SAFE_PREFIX → allow, empty stdout. Proves the fix did not over-block legitimate flows.
`echo 'patch file'`: `patch` is inside a single-quoted arg, not a command token (no start/separator/wrapper before it), so the CMD-anchored patch motif does not match → allow, empty stdout.
